description: This chart compares the precipitation strengthening potential of κ-carbides in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels against conventional microalloying carbides (NbC, VC, TiC) used in microalloyed steels. The x-axis shows precipitation fraction (volume fraction) on a logarithmic scale ranging from 0.0001 to 1, while the y-axis indicates precipitation strengthening in MPa from 0 to 500. The data reveals that κ-carbides provide strengthening between approximately 100 and 350 MPa at relatively high precipitation fractions of 0.05–0.35. In contrast, TiC exhibits the highest strengthening potential (up to ~450 MPa) but at much lower precipitation fractions (~0.0015–0.003), while NbC and VC show intermediate strengthening effects (~70–180 MPa and ~0–120 MPa, respectively) at low volume fractions (~0.0003–0.001). The figure illustrates that although κ-carbides have lower strengthening efficiency per unit volume fraction compared to TiC, they contribute significant age-hardening effects through homogeneous precipitation within austenite at higher volume fractions, which is critical for the strength-ductility balance in Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.
